title: Sargon of Akkad founds the world's first empire
summary: In the twenty-fourth century BCE a Semitic-speaking commoner of obscure birth seized the throne of Kish, marched south, defeated the Sumerian king Lugalzagesi of Uruk, and welded the rival city-states of Mesopotamia into a single political unit ruled from a new capital he built and called Agade. Traditionally dated to about 2334 BCE, Sargon's accession is conventionally treated as the beginning of the Akkadian Empire, the earliest state in history that can plausibly be called an empire in the modern sense: multi-ethnic, multi-lingual, and administered from a centre far larger than any of its parts.

sections.heading: Background
sections.body:
By the middle of the third millennium BCE southern Mesopotamia was a patchwork of Sumerian city-states, each centred on a walled town with its own patron deity, its own king or ensi, and its own quarrels with the neighbours. Ur, Uruk, Lagash, Umma, Nippur, Kish, and Adab warred over irrigation rights, boundary stones, and the prestige of the sacred city of Nippur, whose temple of Enlil was the closest thing the Sumerians had to a national shrine. Slightly to the north lay a linguistically different world, where Semitic-speaking peoples the Sumerians called the Akkadians farmed the middle Euphrates and served as merchants, soldiers, and scribes.

In the generation before Sargon, the ambitious king Lugalzagesi of Umma had briefly conquered most of Sumer, sacking Lagash in a campaign notorious for the temple-burning invective preserved in a curse-tablet of the defeated Lagashite scribe Enannatum. Lugalzagesi installed himself at Uruk and claimed the title 'king of the land'. This was the political situation into which Sargon walked.

Almost nothing is certainly known about Sargon's origins. His own inscriptions call him the son of a gardener; a much later Akkadian text, the so-called Sargon Birth Legend preserved in seventh-century BCE copies from Nineveh, has his mother a priestess who bore him in secret, placed him in a reed basket sealed with bitumen, and set him adrift on the Euphrates, from which he was rescued by a gardener named Akki and eventually rose to become cup-bearer to Ur-Zababa, king of Kish. The story is manifestly a folk tale of divine election, later borrowed for the birth of Moses, and it is impossible to disentangle the historical figure from it. What is not in doubt is that by the last years of Ur-Zababa's reign, Sargon had made himself the strongest man in Kish, and that he acted decisively when the moment came.

sections.heading: What happened
sections.body:
The chronology follows what is called the Middle Chronology, which places Sargon's reign at 2334 to 2279 BCE. Some scholars prefer a Short Chronology shifting these dates roughly a century later, and a Long Chronology placing them earlier; nothing in the argument bears on the substance.

Sargon's first move was to overthrow Ur-Zababa in Kish, whether by coup, assassination, or opportunism during a Sumerian invasion is unclear. He then marched south. Somewhere on the plains he confronted the army of Lugalzagesi of Uruk, defeated it, and captured the king, whom he led in a neck-stock to Nippur and dedicated as a votive offering at the temple of Enlil. Sargon's own inscriptions, preserved in later copies made by scribes in the schools of Nippur and Ur, list the cities he took: Uruk, Ur, E-Ninmar, and Umma among them, with fifty ensis defeated and their walls torn down. He then, according to his inscriptions, washed his weapons in the Persian Gulf, a formulaic way of saying that his authority reached from the mountains to the sea.

Having unified Sumer he turned outward. His annals claim campaigns into Elam in the southeast (modern Khuzestan in Iran), where he sacked Susa and other cities and installed governors; northward up the Tigris into Assyria; westward across the Jazira to the Mediterranean, where he is said to have reached the Cedar Forest of Lebanon and the Silver Mountains of the Taurus; and even, more speculatively, into Anatolia to protect Akkadian merchants at the trading colony of Purushanda. He boasted of 34 victorious battles, of ships from Meluhha (the Indus), Magan (Oman), and Dilmun (Bahrain) docking at his capital, and of ruling a domain that stretched, in the formulaic phrase, 'from the Lower Sea to the Upper Sea', from the Persian Gulf to the Mediterranean.

The capital itself, called Agade or Akkad, was Sargon's own foundation, probably somewhere on the Euphrates north of Nippur. Its precise location has never been identified; despite generations of searching, no excavation has yet produced a site that all Assyriologists agree is Agade. What is certain is that Sargon staffed his new administration with Akkadian-speaking officials and imposed Akkadian, written in cuneiform borrowed from the Sumerians, as an administrative language across the empire. To bind the still-restive Sumerian south to the new order he appointed his daughter Enheduanna as high priestess (en) of the moon-god Nanna at Ur, an office of enormous political and religious weight. The hymns she composed to Inanna, preserved in Old Babylonian scribal copies, make Enheduanna the earliest author, in any language, whose name we know.

sections.heading: Aftermath & consequences
sections.body:
Sargon reigned, if the Sumerian King List can be believed, for 56 years. His last years were marked by revolts across the empire; one text has him besieged in Agade while 'all the lands revolted against him', though he crushed the rebellion. He was succeeded by his son Rimush, who put down further Sumerian revolts with recorded casualty counts running into the tens of thousands, then by another son Manishtushu, and then by Sargon's grandson Naram-Sin, under whom the empire reached its greatest extent. Naram-Sin took the previously unthinkable step of declaring himself a god in his own lifetime, prefixing his name with the divine determinative and depicting himself on the Victory Stele of Naram-Sin (now in the Louvre) wearing the horned crown of divinity.

The Akkadian Empire lasted, in the strict sense, about a century and a half, from Sargon's accession around 2334 to the collapse under Naram-Sin's son Shar-kali-sharri around 2154 BCE. Its collapse was blamed by later Sumerian tradition, in the famous poem 'The Curse of Agade', on Naram-Sin's sacrilegious sack of the temple of Enlil at Nippur, which supposedly brought down the barbarian Gutians from the Zagros mountains as divine punishment. Modern climate proxies, including the well-known 4.2 kiloyear event of severe drought around 2200 BCE, suggest that environmental collapse in the Habur plains of northern Mesopotamia contributed to the empire's fall. Excavations by Harvey Weiss at Tell Leilan in Syria have documented the sudden abandonment of northern agricultural centres during this century.

After a brief and disputed Gutian interlude, Sumerian rule returned under the Third Dynasty of Ur, but the model Sargon had established, of centralised bureaucratic empire administered from a single capital in Akkadian cuneiform, was never truly abandoned. It was inherited by the Old Assyrian and Old Babylonian kingdoms, then by the great Middle and Neo-Assyrian empires of Ashurnasirpal, Sennacherib, and Ashurbanipal, then by the Neo-Babylonian empire of Nebuchadnezzar, and finally by the Achaemenid Persians. In this sense every subsequent Near Eastern empire, and by extension every empire in the western Eurasian tradition, was a distant descendant of what Sargon assembled at Agade.

sections.heading: Legacy
sections.body:
Sargon became for the later ancient Near East what Romulus was to be for Rome: a founder-hero whose name evoked the very idea of legitimate imperial power. His figure recurs in Assyrian and Babylonian omen literature (an eclipse in a particular sign was said to portend 'the years of Sargon'), in the semi-mythological Sargon Legend, in the poem 'King of Battle' recounting his march to distant Purushanda, and in a series of later kings who took his name to advertise their ambitions: Sargon I of Assyria in the nineteenth century BCE and, most famously, Sargon II of Assyria in the eighth century BCE, whose new capital at Dur-Sharrukin near Nineveh was a conscious echo of the lost Agade.

Modern Assyriology has never quite lost interest either. Since Julius Oppert first identified the language of Sargon's inscriptions in the 1850s, the reconstruction of the Akkadian Empire has been one of the great long projects of Mesopotamian archaeology. The Sargon Birth Legend, published in cuneiform by George Smith in the 1870s, drew immediate attention for its parallels to the Moses story in the Book of Exodus. Enheduanna's hymns, first edited by William W. Hallo and J. J. A. van Dijk in 1968, have made Sargon's daughter the subject of a small but vigorous literature on the origins of authorship.

And yet the physical remains of Sargon's empire are stubbornly modest. The city of Agade is still unlocated. The only likely portrait of Sargon himself, a bronze head found in 1931 in a rubbish pit at Nineveh, may in fact represent his grandson Naram-Sin; it was defaced in antiquity, its eyes gouged out, probably during the Median sack of Nineveh in 612 BCE. What survives, above all, is an idea: that many peoples could be ruled as one.

worldContext.text: In Egypt the Old Kingdom was still riding the momentum of the pyramid age. The Fifth and Sixth Dynasties, contemporaries of the Sargonic empire, built smaller pyramids at Abusir and Saqqara whose interior walls were inscribed for the first time with the Pyramid Texts, the oldest religious writings from Egypt. Long-reigning Pepi II sat on the throne for much of Naram-Sin's century; his death around 2181 BCE, coinciding with the same climate disturbance that stressed Mesopotamia, ushered in the fragmentation of the First Intermediate Period.

In prehistoric Europe the copper-using cultures were giving way to the true Bronze Age. On Salisbury Plain the great sarsen circle of Stonehenge stood complete. Across central Europe the Corded Ware complex and, from about 2500 BCE, the Bell Beaker phenomenon spread new burial rites, new metallurgy, and, we now know from ancient DNA studies, considerable movement of people from the Pontic steppe. In the Aegean the Early Cycladic culture of marble figurines and small trading settlements flourished.

The Mature Harappan phase of the Indus Valley civilisation was at its height. Mohenjo-daro, Harappa, Dholavira, Rakhigarhi, and Lothal, with their gridded streets, standardised bricks, elaborate drainage, and public baths, together housed perhaps a million people. Their still-undeciphered seals turn up in Mesopotamian contexts, and Akkadian sources speak of ships from Meluhha docking at the port of Agade. This was probably the busiest long-distance maritime trade route on earth at the time.

In China the late Longshan cultures continued along the Yellow River. At Taosi in southern Shanxi a 280-hectare walled town with graded tombs and what may be an observatory suggests early state formation. Along the middle Yangtze the Shijiahe tradition produced fine jades and elaborate pottery. Chinese tradition places the semi-legendary founder Yu the Great and the beginnings of the Xia dynasty in roughly this era, though archaeologists debate whether the Erlitou culture that appears about 1900 BCE should be identified with historical Xia.

In coastal Peru the Norte Chico civilisation of Caral, Aspero, and Huaricanga was in its last centuries; the great platform mounds would be abandoned around 1800 BCE. In Mesoamerica maize and squash agriculture continued to spread across the highlands and lowlands, and small ceremonial centres were beginning to appear on the Gulf coast that would, a millennium later, produce the Olmec. There is no evidence of writing or state-level organisation anywhere in the Americas at this date.

The islands of Near Oceania, from the Bismarcks through the Solomons, were inhabited by descendants of the founding Pleistocene migrations, farming taro, banana, and yam and speaking languages ancestral to modern Papuan tongues. The Austronesian expansion that would carry farming, pottery (the Lapita tradition), and pigs deep into Remote Oceania was still a thousand years away. Australia remained a continent of hunter-gatherer societies whose intricate rock art traditions were already very ancient.

figures.name: Sargon of Akkad
figures.role: Founder of the Akkadian Empire; reigned c. 2334 to 2279 BCE

figures.name: Enheduanna
figures.role: Daughter of Sargon and high priestess of Nanna at Ur; author of the earliest hymns preserved under a named author's name

figures.name: Lugalzagesi
figures.role: Sumerian king of Umma and Uruk, defeated by Sargon and led captive to Nippur

figures.name: Naram-Sin
figures.role: Grandson of Sargon and greatest king of the Akkadian Empire; first Mesopotamian ruler to claim divinity in his own lifetime

figures.name: Ur-Zababa
figures.role: King of Kish under whom Sargon reportedly served as cup-bearer before seizing the throne

images.caption: The so-called Mask of Sargon, a life-size copper head from Nineveh, sometimes identified as Sargon and sometimes as his grandson Naram-Sin. The eyes were gouged out in antiquity.
